does: This paper introduces ExplainCPE, a benchmark dataset of over 7,000 Chinese pharmacist examination questions requiring free-text explanations. The authors evaluate ChatGPT and GPT-4 on this dataset, analyzing their error patterns and explanation generation capabilities. They identify specific limitations in how current large language models handle textual understanding and computational reasoning tasks within a specialized medical domain. The work additionally reveals that different LLMs exhibit varying preferences for in-context learning strategies, demonstrating how performance varies across model families on the same interpretability task.
connects: This research builds on growing interest in evaluating LLM interpretability beyond simple answer generation, extending prior work on explanation datasets like those used in general knowledge QA benchmarks. However, it addresses a critical gap: existing explanation datasets are predominantly English and cover general knowledge, lacking both linguistic and thematic diversity. The paper responds to calls for more rigorous evaluation of LLMs across non-English languages and specialized domains, combining concerns from medical AI evaluation with broader questions about model interpretability. It complements concurrent research investigating ChatGPT and GPT-4 capabilities across diverse tasks while emphasizing the under-studied dimension of explanation quality in non-English medical contexts.
enables: ExplainCPE establishes a foundation for systematic evaluation of explanation generation in medical AI systems, particularly for non-English-speaking regions where medical LLM deployment is expanding. The benchmark enables future work on improving LLM reasoning transparency in specialized domains and developing better in-context learning strategies tailored to different model architectures. It supports research into cross-lingual and domain-specific interpretability, potentially informing safety practices for deploying LLMs in high-stakes medical settings. The dataset also opens investigation into whether models trained on multilingual data develop equivalent reasoning capabilities across languages and how explanation quality correlates with downstream clinical decision support applications.
